title: Environment Variables for Secrets
subtitle: Keep API keys and passwords out of your code

sections.content:
Here's a mistake that has cost companies millions of dollars: **committing API keys and passwords directly into source code**.

Once a secret is in your git history, it's there forever. Even if you delete it in the next commit, anyone who clones the repo can find it in the history. Bots actively scan GitHub for accidentally committed keys and can exploit them within minutes.

The solution is simple: **environment variables**. Instead of writing secrets in your code, you store them in a `.env` file that never gets committed to git. Your code reads the values at runtime, and your secrets stay safe.
sections.analogy: Think of environment variables like a safe in your office. You don't tape your passwords to your monitor (that's hardcoding secrets in your code). Instead, you lock them in a safe (the .env file) and only take them out when you need them. And you definitely don't photocopy the safe contents and hand them to everyone (committing to git).

sections.instruction: Install the dotenv package. This lets your Node.js app read variables from a .env file automatically.

troubleshooting.solution: You're not in a Node.js project folder. Make sure you're in a directory with a package.json file.

sections.instruction: Create a .env file in the root of your project. This file holds your secrets as key=value pairs, one per line.

troubleshooting.solution: Make sure you have write permissions in the current directory. Try: ls -la to check.

sections.instruction: Load environment variables in your app. The dotenv package reads your .env file and makes everything available through process.env.

blocks.label: app.js — using env vars

sections.explanation: process.env reads from the .env file after dotenv loads it. The || 3000 provides a default value if PORT isn't set — this is a common pattern so your app still works even without a .env file.

sections.question: Why should you NEVER commit a .env file to git?
options: It makes the repository too large
It contains secrets that would be exposed to anyone who clones the repo
.env files only work on your local machine
sections.explanation: Secrets in git history persist forever, even if deleted later. Bots scan GitHub for accidentally committed keys within minutes.

sections.prompt: In Node.js with dotenv, you access an environment variable called API_KEY using `process._____.API_KEY`.
sections.hint: It's a property on the process object that holds all environment variables.
sections.successMessage: Correct — process.env gives you access to all environment variables loaded from .env.

sections.instruction: Environment variable security checklist. These three steps should become second nature for every project you create.

items.hint: Check with: cat .gitignore — you should see .env in the list

items.text: I never log actual secret values
items.hint: Log 'key loaded: Yes' not the key itself. Console output can end up in logs that others read.
So teammates (or your future self) know what variables are needed without seeing the real secrets

completionMessage: You now know how to handle secrets professionally. Environment variables are a non-negotiable part of every real project. .env for secrets, .gitignore to protect them, .env.example to document them.
